definition: Data-center GPU on the Hopper architecture with the Transformer Engine (FP8) for LLM training/inference. Uses HBM3, NVLink, and became the workhorse of the generative-AI boom.
Hopper-generation GPU upgraded with 141 GB of faster HBM3e memory (vs H100's 80 GB), boosting inference throughput and larger-model capacity without changing the core architecture.
NVIDIA's Arm-based, 72-core data-center CPU with LPDDR5X memory, designed to pair tightly with GPUs over NVLink-C2C for AI and HPC — NVIDIA's first datacenter CPU.
A 'superchip' combining a Grace CPU and Hopper GPU on one module via NVLink-C2C, giving the GPU coherent, high-bandwidth access to large CPU memory for big-model workloads.
The architecture after Hopper: a dual-die GPU with second-gen Transformer Engine (FP4/FP6), HBM3e, and 5th-gen NVLink — a major step-up for trillion-parameter model training and inference.
Superchip pairing one Grace CPU with two Blackwell GPUs. The building block of the GB200 NVL72 rack-scale system.
A liquid-cooled rack that links 72 Blackwell GPUs and 36 Grace CPUs over a single NVLink domain so they act as one giant GPU — NVIDIA's rack-scale unit for frontier AI.
Mid-cycle Blackwell refresh with more HBM3e memory and higher inference performance, targeting large-scale reasoning/inference deployments; basis of the GB300 NVL72.
NVIDIA's next custom Arm-based CPU (successor to Grace), built on custom 'Olympus' cores, designed to pair with the Rubin GPU in the Vera Rubin platform.
The GPU architecture after Blackwell (named for astronomer Vera Rubin). Expected to use HBM4 and next-gen NVLink; pairs with the Vera CPU in Vera Rubin systems.
NVIDIA's next rack-scale platform combining Vera CPUs with Rubin GPUs, following the Grace-Blackwell generation on NVIDIA's roughly annual cadence.
NVIDIA's high-bandwidth, low-latency GPU-to-GPU (and GPU-to-CPU) interconnect, far faster than PCIe. Lets multiple GPUs share memory and scale as a tightly coupled unit.
The switch chip that connects many GPUs over NVLink into a full all-to-all fabric, enabling large NVLink domains like the NVL72 to behave as one accelerator.
NVIDIA initiative that opens NVLink to third-party CPUs and custom accelerators (ASICs), letting partners connect their silicon into NVLink-based rack systems.
NVIDIA's baseboard reference design carrying 4-8 GPUs interconnected by NVSwitch. OEMs like HPE build servers around HGX boards for AI training.
A modular server reference architecture that standardizes chassis/mechanicals so OEMs can build many CPU+GPU+DPU configurations quickly across generations.
NVIDIA's own fully integrated AI supercomputer/appliance (hardware + software stack), used as the reference for enterprise AI and the basis of DGX Cloud.
A data processing unit (SmartNIC + Arm cores) that offloads networking, storage, and security from the host CPU — key to zero-trust and 'infrastructure on the NIC.'
NVIDIA's Ethernet networking platform (Spectrum switches + BlueField/ConnectX) tuned for AI clusters, adding congestion control and performance isolation to standard Ethernet.
NVIDIA's InfiniBand switch platform (from Mellanox) offering ultra-low latency and in-network computing (SHARP) for the largest AI/HPC clusters.
NVIDIA's parallel-computing platform and programming model. Its mature software ecosystem and libraries are the primary 'moat' locking developers to NVIDIA GPUs.
NVIDIA Inference Microservices — containerized, optimized model endpoints (part of NVIDIA AI Enterprise) that let enterprises deploy AI models with a standard API.
NVIDIA's supported enterprise AI software suite (frameworks, NIM, tools) sold as a per-GPU subscription and bundled into partner solutions like HPE Private Cloud AI.
